methodology.description: Each SaaS company is scored on six disruption factors (1-10 scale). The composite Disruption Score is a weighted average.

domainExpertiseOverlap.description: How much domain expertise do end users have in the area the software serves? Higher = users can more easily build AI alternatives. 10 = users are developers/technical experts who could vibe-code replacements. 1 = users have no technical ability to create alternatives.
domainExpertiseOverlap.examples: Atlassian (9): devs use it and can build alternatives. Veeva (2): pharma compliance users lack software skills.

smallBusinessExposure.description: What fraction of revenue comes from small businesses vs enterprises? Higher = more SMB exposure = higher disruption risk (SMBs more likely to switch to AI-native tools). 10 = almost entirely SMB. 1 = almost entirely large enterprise.
smallBusinessExposure.examples: Squarespace (9): mostly small businesses. Workday (2): large enterprises.

standardSetterDefensibility.description: Is the company a de facto standard in its domain? Higher = more defensible = LOWER disruption risk. Score is inverted for the composite: 10 = strong standard setter (low risk), 1 = no standard-setting power (high risk).
standardSetterDefensibility.examples: Adobe (9): Creative Suite is the standard. Freshworks (2): competes in crowded space with no standard-setting power.

aiCompetitorPressure.description: How much AI-native competitor pressure does the company face? Higher = more risk. 10 = multiple funded AI-native competitors directly replacing core product. 1 = no AI alternatives exist.
aiCompetitorPressure.examples: Canva (9): AI image generation directly competes. Veeva (2): no AI alternatives in pharma compliance.

switchingCost.description: How high are switching costs? Higher = more lock-in = LOWER disruption risk. Score is inverted for the composite: 10 = deep data/integration lock-in, migration is months of work. 1 = trivial to switch, no data portability issues.
switchingCost.examples: Veeva (10): regulatory data creates massive lock-in. Canva (2): near-zero switching cost.

workflowComplexity.description: How complex are the workflows embedded in org processes? Higher = more complexity = LOWER disruption risk. Score is inverted for the composite: 10 = complex multi-step workflows embedded in org processes. 1 = single-step or commodity task.
workflowComplexity.examples: Veeva (10): FDA-regulated workflows are extremely complex. Zoom (2): video calls are single-step commodity.

methodology.interpretation: Higher composite score = more vulnerable to AI disruption. Range: 1-10.

companies.name: Atlassian
companies.reasoning: End users are developers who can build alternatives. Mix of SMB and enterprise. Jira is a near-standard but alternatives exist. AI coding assistants compete on dev workflow but Jira/Confluence have deep project history lock-in and complex cross-team workflows.

companies.name: Salesforce
companies.reasoning: Sales reps aren't builders. Heavily enterprise. CRM category leader and platform standard. AI CRM tools emerging but immature. Massive data/integration lock-in. Complex multi-department workflows.

companies.name: Adobe
companies.reasoning: Designers are creative but not coders. Mixed customer base. Photoshop/Illustrator are the standard. AI image generation (Midjourney, DALL-E, Firefly) directly compete. Moderate format lock-in. Creative tasks are project-based, not workflow-heavy.

companies.name: Shopify
companies.reasoning: Merchants are business-savvy but not technical. Heavily SMB. Strong brand but Wix/Squarespace compete. AI store builders emerging. Merchant data/payment integrations create lock-in. Order management is moderately complex.

companies.name: Workday
companies.reasoning: HR users aren't technical. Almost entirely large enterprise. Strong position in enterprise HCM. Few AI alternatives for enterprise HCM/finance. Deep payroll/benefits/compliance integrations. Extremely complex regulated workflows.

companies.name: ServiceNow
companies.reasoning: IT admins are technical. Very enterprise-heavy. ITSM standard in large orgs. Limited AI ITSM alternatives. Deep enterprise integrations. Complex multi-tier approval and escalation workflows.

companies.name: Datadog
companies.reasoning: DevOps engineers are highly technical. Mix but enterprise-leaning. Strong but Grafana/New Relic compete. AI observability tools emerging. Dashboard/alert config creates moderate lock-in. Monitoring workflows are structured but not deeply complex.

companies.name: Snowflake
companies.reasoning: Data engineers are very technical. Enterprise-heavy. Strong but Databricks/BigQuery compete. Few AI alternatives to data warehousing. Large data volume migration is painful. ETL pipelines and governance workflows are complex.

companies.name: HubSpot
companies.reasoning: Marketers are savvy but not builders. SMB-focused. Strong in SMB CRM but not a standard. AI marketing/sales tools (Jasper, Clay, etc.) compete directly. Moderate contact data lock-in. Workflows are templated, not deeply complex.

companies.name: Twilio
companies.reasoning: Users are developers building on the API. Mixed customer base. Leading API but competitors exist. Some AI communication alternatives but API-level. Code integration creates lock-in. Communications APIs are functional, not workflow-heavy.

companies.name: Zoom
companies.reasoning: End users are non-technical. Broad customer base. Well-known but Teams/Meet compete hard. AI meeting tools (Otter, Fireflies, Recall) directly compete. Near-zero switching cost. Video calls are single-step commodity.

companies.name: Cloudflare
companies.reasoning: DevOps/infra engineers are highly technical. Mix of SMB and enterprise. Strong edge platform. Few AI alternatives to edge/CDN/security infra. DNS, SSL, WAF rules deeply integrated. Security config is moderately complex.

companies.name: MongoDB
companies.reasoning: Users are developers. Mixed customer base. Popular but Postgres/DynamoDB compete. No AI database replacement. Database migration is very painful. Queries are functional, not workflow-heavy.

companies.name: Figma
companies.reasoning: Designers creative but not coders. Mixed base. Becoming the design collaboration standard. AI design tools (Galileo, Uizard) emerging fast. Some design system lock-in. Design tasks are project-based, relatively simple.

companies.name: Notion
companies.reasoning: Tech-savvy users but not all builders. SMB-leaning. Popular but Google Docs/Confluence compete. AI productivity tools (Mem, Coda AI, etc.) compete. Moderate content lock-in. Docs/wikis are lightly structured.

companies.name: Canva
companies.reasoning: Non-designers using templates. Very SMB. Strong brand but AI image tools are direct competitors. AI image generation directly replaces core use case. No meaningful lock-in. Design tasks are single-step, template-based.

companies.name: Squarespace
companies.reasoning: Non-technical users building websites. Almost all SMB. No standard-setting power. AI website builders (Durable, 10Web, Framer AI) directly compete. Some content lock-in. Website building is simple.

companies.name: GitLab
companies.reasoning: Pure developer tool. Mix but enterprise-leaning. Competes with GitHub. AI coding tools compete on CI/CD and code review. Repo history and pipeline config create lock-in. DevOps pipelines are complex.

companies.name: Veeva Systems
companies.reasoning: Pharma/biotech compliance users aren't technical. All enterprise. Dominant in life sciences vertical. No AI alternatives in pharma compliance. Regulatory data creates massive lock-in. FDA-regulated workflows are extremely complex.

companies.name: Palantir
companies.reasoning: Analysts are semi-technical. Government/enterprise only. Deep moat in defense/intel. No AI alternatives for defense/intel analytics at this scale. Classified data integrations. Complex analytical workflows with security clearance requirements.

companies.name: Freshworks
companies.reasoning: Support agents aren't builders. SMB-heavy. No standard-setting power, crowded market. AI customer support tools directly compete. Low switching cost. Support workflows are templated.

companies.name: Asana
companies.reasoning: Mixed user base, some technical. Mid-market focus. Competes with Monday, Jira, Notion. AI project management emerging. Moderate project data lock-in. Task management is lightly structured.

companies.name: DocuSign
companies.reasoning: Users just sign documents. Mixed base. De facto e-signature standard. Limited AI e-signature alternatives. Compliance/audit trail lock-in. E-signing is a single-step task.

companies.name: Okta
companies.reasoning: IT/security admins are technical. Enterprise-heavy. Strong in identity space. No AI identity alternatives. SSO/directory integrations deeply embedded. Access management policies are complex.

companies.name: Monday.com
companies.reasoning: Non-technical project managers. SMB-leaning. No standard-setting power. AI work management tools emerging. Low data lock-in. Workflows are simple board-based.

companies.name: Elastic
companies.reasoning: Developers and DevOps engineers. Enterprise-leaning. Elasticsearch is well-known but open-source competitors exist. AI search (vector DBs, semantic search) compete partially. Index data and dashboards create lock-in. Search/observability pipelines moderately complex.

companies.name: Wix
companies.reasoning: Non-technical small business owners. Almost all SMB. No standard-setting power. AI website builders directly compete. Some content lock-in. Website building is simple.

companies.name: Zendesk
companies.reasoning: Support agents aren't technical. Mixed base. Known brand but AI chatbots are direct competitors. AI chatbots (Intercom Fin, Ada) directly compete. Ticket history creates moderate lock-in. Support workflows are moderately structured.

companies.name: Confluent
companies.reasoning: Pure developer/data engineer tool. Enterprise-heavy. Kafka is the standard but self-hosted is an option. No AI alternative to data streaming. Kafka deeply embedded in data pipelines. Event-driven architectures are complex.

companies.name: UiPath
companies.reasoning: Business analysts with some technical skill. Enterprise-heavy. RPA leader but AI agents are direct replacements. AI agents are literal replacements for RPA. Moderate bot library lock-in. Automation workflows range from simple to moderately complex.

companies.name: Intuit
companies.reasoning: Small business owners and individuals. Very SMB. QuickBooks/TurboTax are standards. AI bookkeeping tools emerging but tax/compliance is hard to disrupt. Financial data history creates lock-in. Tax workflows are structured and seasonal.

companies.name: Autodesk
companies.reasoning: Engineers are technical but in physical domains. Mixed base. AutoCAD/Revit are industry standards. AI CAD tools emerging but immature for engineering. Large project files and format standards create lock-in. Engineering design workflows are deeply complex.
